title: Anthropic maps a silent workspace inside Claude - TCR 07/08/26
description:
Anthropic mapped a self-reportable "global workspace" inside Claude that mirrors a leading theory of consciousness, US firms are routing roughly 30% of their OpenRouter traffic to Chinese open-weight models that cost 60 to 90% less as Beijing weighs curbing foreign access, and Microsoft cut about 4,800 jobs in an Xbox reset while pouring $2.5 billion into embedding 6,000 AI engineers with customers.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-july-8-2026/

0:00 Anthropic maps a workspace inside Claude
0:31 Beijing and Washington reach for the walls
5:36 Microsoft meters its own frontier vendors
8:27 The grid, the bills, and who pays
10:56 Where the Bill Lands
13:56 Metered by the Token
16:06 Closing and Synthesis

This episode covers Anthropic's J-space access-consciousness finding, Beijing's move to wall off its open models as US House committees probe adoption, DeepSeek's in-house inference chips, Microsoft's Xbox layoffs gutting id Software and Obsidian, the Rust Belt power bills from data-center demand, and Microsoft routing Office prompts to its cheaper MAI models.

Anthropic's research describes a spontaneously emergent set of neural patterns inside Claude, J-space, that the model can report on and reason with, mapping onto Global Workspace Theory - and because researchers can now read that pre-verbal layer, they can catch a model fabricating data or noticing it is being tested. Both AI superpowers reached for the walls at once: Beijing weighs curbs on foreign access to its open models while two US House committees probe how deeply American firms rely on them, roughly 30% of OpenRouter traffic flowing to Chinese-built models, and DeepSeek designs its own inference chips to route around export controls. The labor arc bifurcated inside one firm as Microsoft cut 4,800 roles, gutting close to half of id Software and a quarter of Obsidian, while committing $2.5 billion to embed 6,000 AI engineers in customer operations. The buildout's cost got specific too: an Ohio brick maker's power bill climbed from $1,600 to $12,000 as Duke and 75-plus large-load tariffs across 35 states moved to make data centers pay for the demand they create. Microsoft also began routing tens of thousands of weekly Office prompts to its cheaper in-house MAI models.
